coredata.dc:title: Optoelectronic properties of Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII), GQD/Os-GQD-PXIII nanocomposites, and their coupling with photoanode in dye-sensitized solar cells: Molecular and periodic DFT calculations
coredata.dc:creator: Wazzan, Nuha
Al-Qurashi, Ohoud S.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the optoelectronic properties of a naturally occurring dye, Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII), were investigated. To improve the performance of PXIII dye, it was hybridized with graphene quantum dot (GQD) undoped and doped with Os at the central position to form two nanocomposites (PXIII-GQD and Os-GQD-PXIII), and their optoelectronic properties were also investigated. Parameters such as frontier molecular orbital energies and distributions, absorption and emission properties, excited-state lifetimes, and hole and electron reorganization energies were calculated at two molecular (HCTH and CAM-B3LYP) DFT functionals and their counterparts TDDFT functionals in the gas phase and with the PCM model to mimic the effect of the solvent in three solvents: chloroform, acetonitrile, and water. The variation of the abovementioned molecular parameters as a function of media polarities (dielectric constants) was investigated. In addition, the coupling with the photoanode TiO2 anatase (101) with a periodic DFT functional was also investigated. The output from this work demonstrated the better overall performance of the nanocomposites (doped and undoped). Since they have better energy level alignments, red-shifted and broader absorption in the Vis and IR regions, better HOMO and LUMO charge separation, and stronger adsorption on the photoanode.

head.ce:title: Optoelectronic properties of Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII), GQD/Os-GQD-PXIII nanocomposites, and their coupling with photoanode in dye-sensitized solar cells: Molecular and periodic DFT calculations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the optoelectronic properties of a naturally occurring dye, Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII), were investigated. To improve the performance of PXIII dye, it was hybridized with graphene quantum dot (GQD) undoped and doped with Os at the central position to form two nanocomposites (PXIII-GQD and Os-GQD-PXIII), and their optoelectronic properties were also investigated. Parameters such as frontier molecular orbital energies and distributions, absorption and emission properties, excited-state lifetimes, and hole and electron reorganization energies were calculated at two molecular (HCTH and CAM-B3LYP) DFT functionals and their counterparts TDDFT functionals in the gas phase and with the PCM model to mimic the effect of the solvent in three solvents: chloroform, acetonitrile, and water. The variation of the abovementioned molecular parameters as a function of media polarities (dielectric constants) was investigated. In addition, the coupling with the photoanode TiO2 anatase (101) with a periodic DFT functional was also investigated. The output from this work demonstrated the better overall performance of the nanocomposites (doped and undoped). Since they have better energy level alignments, red-shifted and broader absorption in the Vis and IR regions, better HOMO and LUMO charge separation, and stronger adsorption on the photoanode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Grätzel cell/dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) device [1] has five components; the photoanode, electrolyte, counter electrode (cathode), and photosensitizer. Among these five components, the photosensitizer presents the most important component of a DSSC. The main role of the photosensitizer is to absorb light and inject the photoexcited electrons into the conduction band (CB) of the semiconductor (usually TiO2). The photosensitisers in DSSCs are of three types [2]: (i) metal complexes [3], (ii) organic dyes [4], and (iii) natural dyes [5]. These materials can be molecular-engineered to act more efficiently as photosensitizers in DSSCs.

Naturally occurring dyes can offer a sustainable and renewable option other than synthetic dyes (metal complexes and organic dyes) and thus can be classified as a green chemistry alternative. Naturally occurring dyes extracted from plants are: (1) nature-rich, (2) pollution-free, (3) economical, and (4) easy and straightforward to be separated and refined. These benefits have attracted enormous research interest in DSSC sensitized with naturally occurring dyes [6–8].

Betalains are natural dyes comprised a heteroatomic nitrogen core structure, betalamic acid [4-(2-oxoethylidene)− 1,2,3,4-tetrahydropyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid]. The structures of about seventy-five betalains have been identified from plants of about seventeen families. For more than five decades, a massive amount of research papers has been carried out on betalains. The key factor during the structure determination of betalains was the absorbance spectra in the Ultraviolet-Visible (UVVis) region. All betalains exhibit absorption maxima in both UV and Vis regions due to the phenolic nature of betalamic acid and the conjugated dienes of 1,7-diazaheptamethin substructure, respectively [9]. Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII) is a non-proteinogenic alpha-amino acid, and it is a phytochemical which was extracted from two naturally occurring resources; Portulaca grandiflora [10] and Beta vulgaris L flowers [11]. It was reported that PXIII absorbs light in the Vis region at 470 nm [10].

On the other hand, nowadays, one of the very recent rising stars in materials science is the graphene quantum dot (GQD). The GQDs have been incorporated in various photovoltaic (PV) devices because of their: (i) tuneable band gaps, (ii) big surface area, (iii) environmentally friendly nature, (iv) high electron mobility, (v) excellent ability to absorb UV light and convert it into Vis light, and (vi) suitable material as a sensitizer as an electron/hole transport layer [12]. The introduction of GQDs into the photoactive layer of DSSCs has achieved promising experimental results [13–15].

Though pure GQD [16] faces many obstacles to reaching its total capacity in DSSC, molecular engineering of GQDs tunes the chemical and physical properties, leading to GQD with high performance in DSSC application. The molecular engineering of GQDs included the: (i) functionalization of GQDs, such as oxygenated, halogenated, or nitrogenated the GQD surface (parallel surface or edge plane), (ii) substitutional doping of GQDs with heteroatoms (also known as adatoms technique) [17], (iii) hybrid nanocomposites of GQD with organic [13,14,18–20] or natural dyes [21,22].

It was reported that the hybrid nanocomposites of GQD through covalent interaction with organic or natural dyes is useful for enhancing the optical absorption property, which may provide a new development in optoelectronic devices [23]. Saedi et al. [21] extracted two natural dyes from the marine seaweed, namely red Gracilaria and green Ulva algae. They used them to form nanocomposites with GQD and use the nanocomposites as photosensitizers in DSSCs. The DSSC with GQD-Gracilaria reported an efficiency higher than other organic solar cells. Since it achieved 0.94% power conversion efficiency ( η ), 2.26 mA.cm−2 short-circuit current density (
J
sc
), 0.73 V open-circuit voltage (
V
oc
), and 56% fill factor ( FF ). Mandal et al. [24] studied the GQD- porphyrin nanocomposites DSSCs theoretically. Different GQD- porphyrin nanocomposites were designed either by changing the GQD’s size or by attaching the porphyrin moiety with other functional groups. They prove that the nanocomposites can form type-I/ type-II nanohybrids, and GQD-porphyrin nanohybrid material with larger GQD is a good contender for efficient DSSC. Gao et al. [25] theoretically improved the efficiency of the DSSC by applying the cir-coronene graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD) and tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP) as nanocomposite sensitizer. The first reported cause of improvements is the enhancement of absorbance in the visible (Vis) region. Their results indicated that GQD (18OH)-TPP nanocomposite has the most efficient electrochemical parameters, such as a larger driving force (DF) and larger light-harvesting ability. Another effort by the group of Gao et al. [26], designed theoretically another nanocomposite material, i.e., GQD with carbazole–carbazole dyes. The designed nanocomposites were found to possess the appropriate energy levels, and their absorbance of light fell in the Vis and near IR (NIR) regions. Li et al. [13] did an interesting investigation regarding the possible linkage configuration between the GQD and dye. They tested three linkage types from the calculated photoelectric conversion efficiency and optical and electrical properties. The amidation linkage (amide linkage) was proved to be the most efficient linkage due to the improvement in the absorption intensity and the maximum photon generated current which can decrease the electron recombination rate. In the work of Zhao et al. [18], GQDs were corroborated with different D-π-A type triazatruxene (TAT) based organic dyes. It was found that the GQD/dye possesses a high absorption peak in the UV region, improved molecular molar extinction coefficients, larger light-harvesting efficiency (LHE), and improved charge transfer performance and regeneration ability compared to the isolated dyes. They also successfully predicted the power conversion efficiency ( η ) and other related parameters. One of their designed nanocomposites reached shows a η reached 17.4%. Wang et al. [27] theoretically studied the effect of introducing GQD layer between benzothiadiazole (YKP-88) dye and photoanode (TiO2) on photovoltaic parameters. YKP-88 was anchored on GQD layer by the carboxyl group with a transfer proton to the TiO2 surface. They concluded that GQD increased the amount of charge injection into TiO2 and reduced charge injection time. As well, YKP-88/GQD showed a significant influence on the absorption spectra, which improved
J
sc
and
V
oc
compared to free YKP-88. In the work of Kumara et al. [22], they extracted NDs from the Ixora flower, and they were characterized by UV-Vis, TLC, FTIR, and fluorescence characterization tools, and complete data on the use of these NDs in a photovoltaic device have been presented. The reported power conversion efficiency values of the resultant photosensitized cells are less than 1%, and in the range from 0.203% to 0.951%. Calogero et al. [28] researched anthocyanin and betalain families extracted from wild Sicilian prickly pear, blackberry, red Sicilian orange, mulberry, radicchio, eggplant, and giacche grape as sensitizers for DSSCs application. Results demonstrated that betalain extracted from wild Sicilian prickly pear specie showed η of 2.06%, which is higher than obtained from anthocyanins ( η =1.13%). This is due to a higher oxidation potential of betalain that allows it to interact better with the electrode. As well as the existence of the strong anchoring unit (the carboxylic groups) in the betalain structure. Chanana and Batra [8] studied the solvent effect of twelve solvents with various polarities (dielectric constants ε ) on the molecular properties of two naturally occurring dyes, i.e., purpurin and lawsone molecules using CAM-B3LYP/6–311 + +G(d,p). The study indicates the stability of purpurin is over that of lawsone molecule. Also, it determines how the HOMO and LUMO energy levels affected by solvent polarity. More importantly, they explained the values obtained for light-harvesting efficiency and excited-state lifetimes due to auxochrome effect of the two solvents: aniline, and benzyl alcohol.

Moreover, substitutional doping of GQDs with heteroatoms (dopants) with different electronegativity gives the GQDs more active sites [29]. Other dopants have been incorporated into GQDs, such as boron, oxygen, sulfur, nitrogen, and phosphorus [30]. However, to the best knowledge of the present authors, doping with a transition metal in this context and investigating their effect is not reported yet.

Moreover, the use of suitable solvents is important to improve the efficiency of DSSC. Since the interaction of solvent with the photosensitizer affects the electronic properties such as the absorption and emission spectra [31].

This work is divided into two directions. The first direction is before the adsorption, which included the study with molecular DFT calculations, the potential of isolated systems of PXIII, GQD, and GQD-PXIII nanocomposite as photosensitizers. Coronene (C24H12) is used as the model of hexagonal-shaped GQD [52]. Coronene occurs naturally as the very rare mineral carpathite, which is characterized by flakes of pure coronene embedded in sedimentary rock. The work investigated their frontier molecule energy levels alignments, energies, and distributions, their absorption and emission properties, and intramolecular charge transfer properties. Also, the GQD-PXIII nanocomposite was doped with a transition metal, Osmium metal (Os), and the effect of this dopant on the previously calculated parameters was investigated too. These abovementioned parameters were investigated in media (gas, chloroform, acetonitrile, and water) with different dielectric constants (polarities). The second direction is after the adsorption process, which included the study with periodic DFT calculations, the adsorbed systems on TiO2 anatase (1 0 1). The work involved the calculations of geometrical parameters and adsorption energies of the resultant systems.

The molecular DFT calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 quantum chemistry package [32]. The optimization of geometries of Portulacaxanthin III (PXIII), graphene quantum dot (GQD), GQD-PXIII nanocomposite, transition metal-doped graphene surface (Os-GQD-PXIII) were performed using density functional theory (DFT) with the hybrid exchange-correlation functional CAM-B3LYP [33]. The Pople’s split-valence double-zeta basis set with d and p polarization orbitals and enlarged with one type of diffuse function were chosen, i.e., the 6–31 +G(d,p) basis set for non-metal atoms, while Los Alamos National Laboratory with double zeta (DZ) (LANL2DZ) and pseudopotentials basis set were selected for metal atom Os [34]. The Time-dependent (TD) extension of DFT has been demonstrated to give reliable results for the calculation of the electronic absorption spectra and has been widely used for the prediction of absorption and emission spectra of organic as well as inorganic photosensitizers of the DSSCs [35]. Seven XC-DFT functionals were used to predict the maximum absorption wavelength (
λ
max
calc
) of PXIII dye to see which functional is more capable of reproducing the experimental value (470 nm [10]). The functionals incorporate a different amount of HF exchange. One of the seven functionals are standard pure functionals with no HF exchange, HCTH [36,37], six functionals are standard hybrid-functionals B3LYP (with 20% fraction of HF exchange) [38], APDF [39], HSEH1PBE [40], mPω1Pω91 (25% HF exchange) [41], and two functionals are long-range-corrected functionals CAM-B3LYP (with 15% HF) [42], and ωB97XD (22% HF exchange) [43], within DFT and TD-DFT level of theory. The maximum absorbance wavelength of PXIII dye was calculated at seven XC-DFT functionals with 6–31 +G(d,p) basis set and PCM in water along with the experimental value from ref. [10], and the %deviations between the two sets of data are represented in Fig. 1. As shown in Fig. 1, all the DFT functionals produced maxima absorption wavelengths blueshifted concerning the experimental value. Still, they produced values very close to the experimental one. The percentage deviations (%Dev) between the calculated and experimental value are in the range from 10.2% to 23.0%. The three functionals HSEH1PBE, Mpω1pω91, and APDF gives very similar
λ
max
calc
values and their %Dev equal 18.3%, 18.9%, and 18.3%, respectively. Contrary to our expectation, the two long-range corrected functionals, CAM-B3LYP and ωB97XD, give the largest deviations from experiment (22.9%, and 23.0%, respectively). The B3LYP gives an intermediate deviation between the other estimated values (%Dev = 16.9%). The functional HCTH gives the most accurate prediction (%Dev = 10.2%). Therefore, this functional is used to simulate the absorption and the subsequent emission spectra of this dye and its nanocomposites. This theoretical approach should result in similar accuracy of the computed wavelengths, Section 3.1.2.

Therefore, using standard pure functional, HCTH, the UV−Vis absorption (for 50 excited states) and emission (from 12 excited states) spectra were predicted. A polarizablecontinuum model (PCM) [44] using water, acetonitrile, and chloroform solvents were implemented to simulate the solvent effect. The PCM is widely used to treat solvent effects on molecular properties and includes non-electrostatic effects such as dispersion and repulsion interactions. In the PCM model, the solvent is treated as a dielectric-medium continuum. The solvation process is accompanied by free energy exchange with the environment, and this free energy is obtained by:

Molecular visualizations were performed using GaussView program (version 5.0.8) [57]. The molecular electrostatic potential maps (MESPs) were drawn using the Avogadro package [58].

The Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) was used for periodic DFT calculations [59]. As widely used and validated in literature [24,60,61], the projector augmented plane waves with the generalized gradient-correlated approximation (GGA), and the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation functional were carried out to optimize the PXIII@TiO2, GQD-PXIII@TiO2, and doping system Os-GQD-PXIII@TiO2 [62]. TiO2 anatase (101) surface was built with a 4 × 5 supercell. The energy cut-off and the convergence threshold for self-consistent iteration are set to 400 eV and
10
− 4
, respectively. The DFTD3 model [63] has been used to incorporate van der Waals interactions. In addition, onsite Ti 3d electrons Coulombic repulsion (U) was considered by PBE+U. The relationship between computational approaches and electronic properties of dye@TiO2 systems has been studied [64–66]. Concerning the Coulombic repulsion potential, it was discovered that U values between 3.5 and 10 eV generated band gaps in realistic agreement with the experiment. Additionally, U = 8.5 eV was chosen for titanium atoms in previous studies [67–70]. Thus, in this study, the same value (8.5 eV) was used. The Gamma point in the reciprocal space was used in the geometric relaxation process to reduce the calculation consumption. On the other hand, the Monhkorst-Pack 4 × 4×1 was chosen for the adsorption systems [71] to obtain the electronic structures of the optimized geometry configurations.

The optimized geometries of the investigated isolated molecules PXIII and GQD (coronene (C24H12) as the model of hexagonal-shaped GQD [72]) at PCM/CAM-B3LYP with 6–31 +G(d,p) in water are depicted in Fig. 2. As calculated by the present authors, GQD is not a dye as it absorbs light only in the UV region [73] since all the absorption for the estimated 50 excited states appeared in the UV region. The two maxima absorption wavelengths are
λ
max
abs
= 338.26 and 216.68 nm, with oscillation strength (
f
max
abs
)
equal 0.6962 and 0.7454, respectively. The solar spectrum usually occurs in the Vis and near-infrared region (NIR) [74], the absorption of GQD in the UV region did not prefer its use as a photosensitizer in DSSC devices since this absorption will not match the solar spectrum [75]. Therefore, the optoelectronic properties were calculated only for the PXIII and the two nanocomposites resulting from its hybridization with GQD and Os-GQD. Even though the calculated HOMO and LUMO energy levels (
E
HOMO
, and
E
LUMO
, respectively) of GQD equal − 5.990 and − 0.767 eV, respectively, at PCM/CAM-B3LYP/6–31 +G(d,p) in water. Those energy levels are aligned properly with those of the conduction band (CB) of the common TiO2 semiconductor (−3.74 eV) and redox potential (RP) of the common iodine electrolyte shuttle (−4.8 eV). More importantly, the calculated energy gap (
Δ
L − H
=
E
LUMO
−
E
HOMO
)
equals 5.223 eV in good agreement with the reported value ( ∼ 5.9 eV) in ref. [76] obtained using thermally-assisted-occupation DFT (TAO-DFT), which is a very effective method for investigating nanoscale systems possess strong static correlation effects.
The doping of GQD with the Os atom was performed by replacing a central carbon atom in GQD by an Os atom.

The binding energies of the two nanocomposites GQD-PXIII are positive indicating the stability of the resultant nanocomposites, see Table 1. The binding energies of GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII equal 6.595 and 6.682 eV, respectively. The binding energy of doped GQD-PXIII is larger than that of undoped GQD by 0.087 eV, indicating the effect of doping with the Os atom in a slight stabilizing of the resultant system.

To confirm the effect of doping on the electronic properties of GQD-PXIII nanocomposite, the wark function
E
WF
of doped and undoped nanocomposites have been calculated. Work function is the minimum energy required by an electron present in the highest filled level to jump outside the solid at a field-free zone at an absolute temperature. The calculated
E
WF
values of the two nanocomposites are comparable to the reported ones for similar GQDs [17].
E
WF
value of undoped GQD equals 3.838 eV, which is slightly unaffected by the doping with Os atom (3.791 eV), the difference is 0.047 eV. The slight decrease in the
E
WF
value due to the doping process can be used to explain the transfer of charge between GQD-PXIII and the dopant (Os atom). In addition, the effect of doping on the molecular dipole moment is significant. Since the dipole moment of GQD-PXIII is 6.49 Debye while it is increased by 17.43 Debye for the Os-GQD-PXIII nanocomposite, this can be ascribed to the change of structure, which creates a difference between local dipoles due to replacing of Carbon (C) atom by Os atom. Also, the slight difference in electronegativity of C (2.5) and Os (2.2) may result in charge redistribution and contribute to the formation of dipole moment [17,77]. The photosensitizer should fill two essential requirements to fulfill the criteria to be used as a light harvester in a DSSC device: (i) it should absorb the solar energy intensely and in the appropriate region of the solar spectrum (range from visible to infrared wavelength of the sunlight because most energy of the sunlight is included in this light region) [78], and (ii) it should have the proper frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) alignment. The following two sections, Section 3.1.2 and Section 3.1.3, will discuss these properties for PXIII, doped and undoped nanocomposites in four media with different polarities.

The FMOs are mainly referred to as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The FMOs of a photosensitizer should be appropriately aligned with the other main two components in the DSSC, i.e., the semiconductor and electrolyte shuttle. The LUMO should be less stable (destabilized) with respect to the conduction band (CB) of the semiconductors (such as TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, Fe2O3, and WO3, their CBs equal −3.75, −4.90, −4.29, −4.85, and −4.60 eV, respectively) [79]. While the HOMO of the photosensitizer should be more stable (stabilized) with respect to the redox potential (RP) of electrolytes (such as
I
−
/
I
3
−
, and
Br
−
/
Br
3
−
, their RPs equal −4.8 eV/−0.53 V [80–82], and −1.9 V [81], respectively). The good alignment of LUMO with the CB of the semiconductor ensures that the injection of the photoexcited electron from the photosensitizer to the CB is thermodynamically favorable, while the good alignment of HOMO with the RP of the electrolyte ensures that the dye regeneration process is thermodynamically favourable. Therefore, from a thermodynamic point of view, as much as the LUMO and HOMO energy levels are closer to the CB and RP, respectively, as much as the processes of electron injection and dye regeneration will be easier. Fig. 3 and Table 2 show the energy levels alignment of HOMOs and LUMOs of the investigated PXIII, GQD-PXIII nanocomposite, Os-GQD-PXIII (doped nanocomposite) calculated at CAM-B3LYP with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ basis sets in the gas phase. The energy levels alignment in Fig. 3 indicates all the systems under investigation are energetically favorable for DSSCs utilizing different semiconductors and
I
−
/
I
3
−
based redox shuttle. The LUMOs energy levels of PXIII, GQD-PXIII, Os-GQD-PXIII equal − 1.352, − 1.519, and − 1.830 eV, respectively, less stable than the CB (−3.75 eV) of TiO2 (the most common applied semiconductor). Therefore, the investigated compounds have the proper HOMO energy levels. Moreover, the difference between those values and CB (
Δ
E
L − CB
)
are 2.388, 2.221, and 1.910 eV, respectively. The electron will be injected into the CB but will be injected more smoothly (as the difference becomes smaller) in the following order: PXIII (2.388 eV) > GQD-PXIII (2.231 eV) > Os-GQD-PXIII (1.910 eV). The two systems of nanocomposites, especially the doped one, show the most feasible electron injection process. On the other hand, the HOMO energy levels of the investigated compounds are compatible with the RP level (−4.8 eV) of
I
−
/
I
3
−
(the most used electrolyte shuttle). The HOMO energy levels of PXIII, GQD-PXIII, Os-GQD-PXIII equal − 7.223, − 6.156, and − 5.752 eV, respectively. The investigated compounds show a feasible dye regeneration process. The feasibility of dye regeneration can also be deduced from the difference between the HOMO energy levels and the RP (
Δ
E
RP − H
)
and it follows the order: PXIII (2.423 eV) < GQD-PXIII (1.356 eV) < Os-GQD-PXIII (0.952 eV). Also, the nanocomposites, especially the doped ones show the most feasible dye regeneration process.

In DSSCs, the energy gap (
∆ E
L − H
) is one of the key parameters in determining the photocurrent density of the photosensitizer. Generally, minimal energy gaps ease electron excitation and are thus helpful in absorbing light at a longer wavelength. Therefore, at any time, photons are absorbed more intensely. This contributes to obtaining a higher
J
sc
and η
[83]. A minimal energy gap indicates that minimal energy is required for electron excitation from the ground state to the excited state, thus harvesting additional sunlight. The energy gaps follow the order of PXIII (5.871 eV) > GQD-PXIII (4.637 eV) > Os-GQD-PXIII (3.922 eV). Therefore. The construction of nanocomposite between PXIII and GQD results in lowering the energy gap by 21% and 24% for GQD-PXIII and Os-GQD-PXIII, respectively, compared to the energy gap of isolated PXIII dye. In addition, the doping GQD-PXIII with Os results in a further decrease in the energy gap by 15% compared to that of the undoped system.

The electron density distributions over HOMO and LUMO are represented graphically in Fig. 4. For PXIII, the electron density of HOMO and LUMO orbitals is delocalized on the skeleton of the molecule. Therefore, the distribution of the two molecular orbitals is quite similar. On the other hand, the hybridized nanocomposite showed a different distribution of HOMO and LUMO orbitals. Therefore, the hybridization results in the redistribution of FMOs over various parts in molecules. The electron density of HOMO orbitals in GQD-PXIII and Os- GQD-PXIII is delocalized mainly over the GQD unit. In contrast, the LUMO orbital is principally spread over the dye molecule. Few residues are delocalized on GQD unit. The delocalization of LUMOs over the dye molecule is expected to strengthen the dye-TiO2 electronic coupling resulting in an effective electron injection process [69,84]. Though transition occurs from HOMO to LUMO, the electron density transfer from the GQD unit to the dye is useful for charge injection from the dye to the semiconductor, see Section 3.2. Another notice, the electron density of HOMO is equally delocalized on the GQD-PXIII, while there is a significant delocalization on the Os and its neighboring atoms in Os-GQD-PXIII, the available filled d-orbital of Os transition metal allows the occupation of electronic density this part of doped GQD unit. Also, the vacant d-orbital of Os carries a small fraction of LUMO orbital, see Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of medium polarity on FMOs
ce:section.ce:para:
Table 2 listed the HOMO and LUMO energy levels and their gaps of PXIII, GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII in media of polarities (different dielectric constants), the gas phase (
ε = 0 )
, chloroform (
ε = 4.71 )
, acetonitrile (
ε = 35.69 )
, and water (
ε = 78.4 )
calculated at CAM-B3LYP and PCM/CAM-B3LYP with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ basis sets. The data are represented graphically in Fig. 5. The solvent particularly polar ones are expected to affect the electronic properties of molecules through long-range interaction [85]. As we can see from Table 2 and Fig. 5, the HOMO energy levels of PXIII dye decrease (destabilized; less negative) in the following order: gas > water > acetonitrile > chloroform. While for GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII, the order is: chloroform > acetonitrile > water > gas. This trend, in turn, will appear in the values of
Δ
E
RP − H
(decrease) and the dye regeneration process will be more feasible in the reverse order. On the other hand, the LUMO energy levels of PXIII will increase (stabilized; more negative) in the order: chloroform < acetonitrile < water < gas. While for the nanocomposites (undoped and doped), the order is: water < acetonitrile < gas < chloroform, therefore,
Δ
E
L − CB
will decrease, and the feasibility of the electron injection process will follow the same order.

Regarding the
∆ E
L − H
values for PXIII dye decrease -and thus be beneficial for easier electronic excitation and light-harvesting processes- in the following order: chloroform > acetonitrile > water > gas. While for GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII, the values in chloroform, acetonitrile, and water is much smaller than that in the gas phase. Therefore, for the two nanocomposites, the solvent effect induces larger
Δ
E
RP − H
and smaller
Δ
E
L − CB
as a result of the stabilization of LUMO and HOMO orbitals [85]. Also, for the two nanocomposites, and generally, the solvent effect induces smaller energy gaps, and therefore red-shift of the absorption is expected as compared with that in the gas phase.

Table 3 lists the maximum absorption wavelengths
λ
max
abs
, oscillator strengths (
f
abs
), light harvesting efficiency (LHE), excited-state lifetimes ( τ ), and short-circuit current density (
J
sc
) calculated at CPM/TDHCHC/6–31 G+ (d,p) and LANL2DZ level of theory for PXIII, GQD-PXIII, Os-GQD-PXIII in water, the data are represented graphically in Fig. 6. It is essential to perform our basic calculations in water solvent since the available experimental data for PXIII is in water [10]. Using GQDs as sensitizers in DSSC is known to have a red shift (panchromatic) effect [86]. According to the calculated data and consistent with the experimental data as discussed in Section 2.1, PXIII absorbed intensely in the Vis region with
λ
max
abs
= 422.28 nm. This transition is mainly due to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). Another two less intense peaks appeared in the UV region, with
λ
abs
= 266.01, and 203.61 nm, occurred due to π→π * transition. The intensities of the three absorption peaks are measured by the parameter the oscillation strength
(
f
abs
),
f
abs
= 0.5984, 0.3149, and 0.061 for the three peaks, respectively. The hybrid between PXIII and GQD to form GQD-PXIII nanocomposite results in a significant red-shift of the absorbance in the Vis region,
λ
max
abs
= 458.42 nm also, the other peaks in the UV region are red-shifted with a peak that has
λ
abs
=
348.01 nm. The intensities of the two peaks are comparable and equal to 0.6792 and 0.6748, respectively, more intense than those of PXIII dye. On the other hand, the dopped nanocomposite (Os-GQD-PXIII) absorbs light less intensely (
f
abs
= 0.240) at very longer wavelength (
λ
max
abs
= 1416.02 nm). Another two peaks appeared in the absorption spectrum of Os-GQD-PXIII, one in the Vis region at 585.77 nm red-shifted with respect to that of PXIII by more than 163 nm but blueshifted with respect to that of undoped nanocomposite by only 6 nm. The second peak belongs to the UV region, and it is also red-shifted with respect to that of PXIII and undoped nanocomposite by 57 and 17 nm, respectively. The intensities of these two bands are less than those of the undoped GQD-PXIII nanocomposite. However, the appearance of a peak at 1416.02 nm of doped GQD-PXIII qualifies this nanocomposite as an efficient photosensitizer in solar cell applications. In addition, as we can see from Fig. 6 and as a criterion for a dye to act as an efficient photosensitizer, the absorption spectrum of Os-GQD-PXIIII nanocomposite is significantly broad and covers a wide region of the electromagnetic spectrum (200–1800 nm), and thus this nanocomposite will be able to absorb light (with different intensity), and this will improve the light-harvesting efficiency over a large region of the solar spectrum. In contrast, the absorption properties of GQD-PXIII are better than those of the isolated PXIII dye since the GQD-PXIII has a broader absorption (200 to ∼ 1000 nm) compared to that of PXIII (200 to ∼ 600 nm).

The light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) plays a vital role in controlling the efficiency of DSSCs. LHE of a photosensitizer denotes the light-catching ability of DSSC cells, as LHE is strictly related to the photocurrent response [45]. We can notice that the efficiency of DSSCs sensitized to the systems under investigation is similar since their LHEs are comparable. However, we should mention that the LHEs are not so impressive since the maximum value reaches only 79.1%.

The short-circuit current density (
J
sc
) is an essential parameter to establish the photoelectric conversion efficiency ( η ) of DSSCs. Generally,
J
sc
largely affected by the
λ
max
abs
and LHE . The estimated
J
sc
values are listed in Table 3. The
J
sc
values are estimated to follow the order of PXIII (1.14 mA cm−2) < GQD-PXIII (11.08 mA cm−2) < Os-GQD-PXIII (13.07 mA cm−2). The order of estimated short-circuit current densities can be correlated very well with the absorption properties of the photosensitizers. As we pointed out above, the GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII with broader absorption properties cover some and the whole solar spectrum, respectively, facilitated the increase in their
J
sc
values. In addition, the PXIII dye possesses a significantly smaller
J
sc
value, owing to its narrow absorbance. Therefore, the two nanocomposites possess broader absorbance to have a significant increase in the
J
sc
values. The big difference in
J
sc
values of isolated dye and hybridized systems will, in turn, expected to dramatically affect η of the resultant DSSC devise. The DSSC photosensitized with PXIII isolated dye is expected to have a smaller η value compared to DSSC photosensitized with GQD-PXIII/Os-GQD-PXIII nanocomposites.

In optoelectronics, to evaluate how the electron is injected efficiently from dye molecule to TiO2, it is beneficial to estimate the excited-state lifetime ( τ ). The electron injection process occurred very fast; within 100 femtoseconds (fs), the electron was transferred (injected) from the excited dye molecule to the TiO2 semiconductor. After this process, the dye will exist in its cationic form before it is regenerated by the electrolyte shuttle occurs. The dye-regeneration process occurred within 100 ns–1
μ s
. For long-term stability, the dye must remain stable in the cationic form for a longer time, i.e., when the excited state lifetime of the dye molecule is large [83]. As reported from some porphyrin-based DSSCs, the smaller electron lifetime resulted in a remarkable decrease in open-circuit voltage (
V
oc
) compared to Ru sensitizer N719 [87]. To calculate the excited-state lifetimes, the geometries of the first excited singlet electronic states were optimized. The lowest excitation energy and corresponding oscillator strength were obtained with the PCM/TDHCTH with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ in water.

Table 3 lists the values of τ of the systems under investigation. For the main absorption peak), the τ values increase in the order: PXIII (7.817 ns) < GQD-PXIII (38.689 ns) < Os-GQD-PXIII (58.788 ns). Therefore, the hybridization of PXIII dye with GQD and Os-GQD enhanced the charge transfer efficiency and, accordingly, the efficiency of the DSSC sensitized with these nanocomposites. Moreover, the doped GQD has a better ability to transfer charge than the undoped GQD, the absorption in the IR region (1416.02 nm) has a significantly longer excited-state lifetime of 122.495 ns.

The electron density difference between the first excited state and ground state was mapped to illustrate intramolecular charge transfer, Fig. 7 visually. The cyan colour indicates the depletion of electron density, and the blue colour indicates the enlargement of electron density. From Fig. 7, it is evident that for the PXIII dye, the maximum electron density is distributed on the whole skeleton of the molecule except for parts of the carboxylic units. On the other hand, the GQD doped and undoped exhibit the maximum electron density in the two nanocomposites, and the dye molecule exhibits the minimum electron density. Hence, it can be concluded that the charge is transferred from the photosensitizer to the GQD units during the electronic transition.

From the first excited state optimized geometries, the emission properties were computed using the time-dependent (TD) density functional theory (DFT) method. Table 4 lists the maximum emission wavelengths (
λ
max
EM
), oscillator strengths (
f
max
EM
), and the Stokes shifts (
λ
ss
) calculated at CPM/TDHCTH with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ for PXIII, GQD-PXIII, Os-GQD-PXIII in water. All systems under investigation emit/fluorescence at longer wavelengths than their absorption wavelengths. Therefore, there is a significant red-shift in the emission wavelengths represented by the Stocks shift (
λ
ss
) values,
λ
ss
values range from 124.10 to 427.64 nm. The bathochromic (red-shift) in the emission wavelength with maximum
λ
ss
value indicate stronger intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect in the excited state [84,88]. This is the case of doped GQD-PXIII nanocomposite, followed by the undoped GQD-PXIII, then the isolated dye PXIII. The emission spectrum of Os-GQD-PXIII is broad with two maxima at 921.97 and 1760.44 nm, corresponding to the two absorbance peaks at 585.77 and 1416.02 nm. Fig. 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Effect of medium polarity on LHE, τ , and
λ
ss
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of DSSC is affected by the solvent used to extract the natural dye [8]. Hence, it is crucial to recognize the effect of a medium in changing the electronic properties of dye molecules. In this direction, the absorption properties represented by the LHE parameter were calculated at TDHCTH and PCM/TDHCTH with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ in media of different dielectric constants (polarities): the gas phase (
ε = 0 )
, chloroform (
ε = 4.71 )
, acetonitrile (ACN) (
ε = 35.69 )
, and water (
ε = 78.4 )
for the three systems under investigation. The data are collected in Table 5. On the other hand, Fig. 9 showed the LHEs of peaks of maxima intensity of PXIII (P3), GQD-PXIII (P2), and Os-GQD-PXIII (Peaks: P2, P3) as a function of medium dielectric constant. The
λ
max
abs
values in the three solvents are red-shifted compared to those in the gas phase. Thus, solvents improved the absorption properties of these photosensitizers. The LHE of a molecule is dependent on the calculated oscillator strength ( f ) [8]. For the PXIII and GQD-PXIII, we considered in Fig. 9 only one peak (P3/P2), the one with maximum intensity, and appeared in the Vis region. The maxima LHE values of PXIII and GQD-PXIII appear in the chloroform, and they are 76.83% and 84.37%, respectively. While the minima LHE values appeared in acetonitrile for PXIII (75.17%) and in the gas phase for GQD-PXIII (46.48%). We considered two peaks for the doped nanocomposite (Os-GQD-PXIII) since the two peaks are important (in Vis and IR regions). The LHE values as a function of media dielectric constants is similar for the two peaks. The largest LHE is in the gas phase. The effect of solvent dielectric constants (polarity) on the LHE values is different. On the other hand, among the investigated systems, GQD-PXIII nanocomposite recorded the maximum LHE (84.4%) in chloroform, corresponding to absorbance at 596.41 nm.

Table 5 listed the excited-state lifetimes τ calculated at TDHCTH and PCM/TDHCTH with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ in media with different polarities for the three investigated dye/nanocomposites. Additionally, Fig. 9 showed the τ values as a function of medium dielectric constant. As we can see from Table 5 and Fig. 9, generally, when moving from the gas phase to solvent, the excited state lifetimes differ significantly. Also, the excited-state lifetimes of PXIII (P3), GQD-PXIII (P2), and Os-GQD-PXIII (P2 and P3) in the two polar solvents (larger ε values), i.e., ACN and water are very comparable. Whereas, the τ values in less polar solvent (chloroform) are significantly different, the exception is PXIII where the τ values in the three solvents are very comparable, which indicate that in the case of isolated dye the solvent results in similar effect on the modifications of the ground state upon excitation [65].

Table 5 listed the values of Stokes shifts
λ
ss
calculated at TDHCTH and PCM/TDHCTH with 6–31 +G(d,p) and LANL2DZ in media with different polarities for the three investigated dye/nanocomposites. Additionally, Fig. 9 shows the
λ
ss
values as a function of medium dielectric constant. The
λ
ss
values of PXIII increase as the medium polarity increases, while for GQD-PXIII,
λ
ss
value in the gas phase is the maximum, and then it drops significantly in chloroform and then increases gradually as the solvent polarity increases. Regarding the doped GQD-PXIII, the
λ
ss
values of the two peaks in ACN and water are very similar and are maxima, while P2 shows minimum
λ
ss
value in the gas phase, the minimum value for P3 is in chloroform. In conclusion, the properties of solvents must be considered for engineering DSSC cells.

The reorganization energy λ is a product of two parameters: the intermolecular reorganization energy (contribution from the medium polarization energy) [51], and the intramolecular reorganization energy. The intermolecular reorganization energy has an insignificant effect on the charge/electron transfer rate. Intramolecular reorganization energy (= reorganization energy, hereafter) is the difference in energy because of the alteration of geometry as it goes from a charged to a neutral state and from a neutral to a charged state. It can be seen from Eq. 4 the smaller the λ term, the smaller the geometry relaxations, and the larger the
K
CT
.
The intramolecular reorganization energy can be expressed by Eq. 9 and Eq. 10 [52–55].

The calculated electron and hole reorganization energies (λelec, and λhole, respectively ) in media of different polarities of the systems under investigation are summarized in Table 6 and represented graphically in Fig. 10. For the efficient performance of a solar cell, λ value should be as small as possible. This will enhance the charge transfer process, and thus better electronic injection into the TiO2 conduction band will be obtained.

We should mention here that most of the hole/electron reorganization energies of the three systems can be considered high values compared to those reported in the literature for other transporter materials (0.2–0.3 eV) [89], which implies that the investigated dye/nanocomposites are weaker hole and electron transporters. There is a direct and strong relationship between the
J
sc
value and
λ
elec
[90]. It can be seen from Table 6 and Fig. 10 that the hole and electron reorganization energies relationship varies with the applied medium. If the hole reorganization energy is smaller than the electron reorganization energy, the dye/nanocomposite will be better hole transporter than the electron transporter, and vice versa [91]. The hole reorganization energies decrease in the following order:
Gas: GQD-PXIII > PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII.
Chloroform: GQD-PXIII > PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII
Acetonitrile: PXIII > GQD-PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII.
Water: GQD-PXIII > PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII.

Thus, the doped nanocomposite showed the best hole-transporting ability in all media. The electron reorganization energies decrease in the following order:
Gas: PXIII > GQD-PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII.
Chloroform: PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII > GQD-PXIII.
Acetonitrile: PXIII > GQD-PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII.
Water: PXIII > Os-GQD-PXIII > GQD-PXIII.

Therefore, the nanocomposites showed better electron reorganization energies than that of the isolated dye PXIII. In addition,
λ
elec
values are smaller (and better) for Os-GQD-PXIII in gas and acetonitrile media, and these values are smaller and better for GQD-PXIII nanocomposite in chloroform and water media. For GQD-PXIII, we can note that
λ
elec
<
λ
hole
in all media, GQD-PXIII is better as an electron transporter than as a hole transporter. The doping of this nanocomposite results in altering the electron and hole transporting properties, since for Os-GQD-PXIII,
λ
hole
<
λ
elec
. This can be attributed to the effect of the vacant d-orbital of the Os atom that could ease the hole mobility more than the electron mobility. On the other hand, the hole transporter ability of PXIII dye is more than its electron transport ability in the three solvents.

It is a fact that the interface structure between the photosensitizer and TiO2 (the typical semiconductor) determines the interface energy alignment. It thus influences the photovoltaic property and cell performance of the DSSCs device [92]. The adsorption energies of dye/nanocomposite@TiO2 are important from two points: (1) it is determined the strength of interaction between dye/nanocomposite and the TiO2 surface, and (2) it determines the electron transfer rate (
k
et
) ,
i.e., as the adsorption energy becomes larger as the electronic coupling between the dye molecule (anchoring unit) and TiO2 becomes stronger, and consequently, the larger the
k
et
value is. In our calculations, the (TiO2)63 anatase (101) (abbreviated as TiO2 (101)) surface supercell for monomer adsorption, anatase is the most used phase of TiO2 due to its superior charge transport. The optimized photosensitizer (PXIII/GQD-PXIII/Os-GQD-PXIII) is bound to the TiO2 substrate implying the bidentate bridging mode. In this mode of adsorption, the two Oxygen atoms of the carboxylic group of the PXIII dye molecule are bonded to two Ti atoms, and the protonated H atom in the hydroxyl group is transferred and bonded to one of the nearby Oxygen atoms on the surface of TiO2, see Fig. 11. Some bond distances such as Ti–O/Os–C, adsorption energies
(
E
ads
), and Fermi energies (
E
Fermi
) are listed in Table 7. The recorded bond distances range from 1.8976 to 2.0989 Å, fall in a small range, and are very similar to the reported values. The bidentate bridging mode provides stable adsorption configurations with adsorption energies (
E
ads
) equal − 1.679, − 1.688, and − 1.880 eV for PXIII@ TiO2, GQD-PXIII@TiO2, and Os-GQD-PXIII@TiO2 adsorbed systems, respectively. Negative adsorption energy indicates that these photosensitizers can be stably adsorbed on the TiO2 surface, and the more negative adsorption energy the more stable adsorbed system. Thereby, the stability of the adsorbed systems can be arranged in the order: Os-GQD-PXIII@TiO2 > GQD-PXIII@TiO2 > PXIII@TiO2. The hybrid adsorbed systems have smaller
E
ads
values compared to isolated dye-adsorbed systems. Additionally, the doped nanocomposite with Os has the least
E
ads
value. The hybridization of PXIII dye with GQD is an efficient way to increase the stability of the resultant adsorbed system. This may refer to a charge redistribution within the framework of the nanocomposite, which leads to better electron coupling with TiO2 semiconductor and a more stable adsorbed system.

The electronic structure of the free anatase TiO2(101) surface and dye/nanocomposite systems has been calculated, and we did not consider the partial DOS (PDOS) from each component in dye/nanocomposites@TiO2 systems, i.e., dye and TiO2, because the PDOS of dye is expected to have a minor contribution to the TDOS [92], Fig. 12. As shown in Fig. 12, the valence band (VB) state consists mainly of oxygen (p-orbital), whereas the conduction band is formed by titanium (d-orbital).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular electrostatic potentials maps of dye and nanocomposites (isolated and adsorbed)
ce:section.ce:para:
In DSSCs, the interaction of the dye with TiO2 is a crucial factor that affects open-circuit voltage which can be realized by calculating the molecular electrostatic potential (MESP). It provides qualitative insight into the way charges are transferred from donor to acceptor through electrostatic potentials. The molecular electrostatic potential at a given point
p ( x , y , z )
in the vicinity of a molecule is the force acting on a positive test charge (a proton) located at p through the electrical charge cloud generated through the molecule’s electrons and nuclei. Even though the molecular charge distribution remains unperturbed through the external test charge (no polarization occurs), the electrostatic potential of a molecule is still a good guide in assessing the reactivity of the molecule towards positively or negatively charged reactants. The MESP map can be defined as the potential experience of a unit positive charge in the vicinity of the molecule due to its electron density distribution [17].

MESP shows the chemical reactivity as positive potential, which can be acted as the site for the electrophilic attack. In contrast, the negative potential can be acted as a site for nucleophilic attack. Fig. 13 shows the MESPs of the investigated dye and nanocomposites in their isolated and adsorbed forms. The MESP is mapped on the surface of an electron density of 0.1 au. The negative electrostatic potential is red color while the positive electrostatic potential is represented by blue color. In addition, the white colour shows a region with zero potential.

The negative electrostatic potentials are distributed as expected on the more electronegative atoms (Oxygen and Nitrogen atoms) of PXIII dye. The nanocomposites are also located on the GQD unit but are more pronounced on the Os-GQD unit. The most important note is the positive electrostatic potentials found on the intersection region between the dye/nanocomposite and TiO2 surface. Zero/negative electrostatic potentials surround this region on dye, GQD Os-GQD, and other regions of TiO2. This charge separation indicates an efficient electronic coupling between the photosensitizer and TiO2 surface.

In this work, the naturally occurring PXIII dye was hybridized with undoped GQD and doped Os-GQD to form two novel nanocomposites. The DFT and TDDFT calculations were implemented to investigate nanocomposites as better photosensitizers in DSSC than the isolated dye. The hybrid between PXIII and GQD to form GQD-PXIII nanocomposite exhibited better energy level alignments and affected the distribution of FMOs over various parts in PXIII. Also, it decreased
∆ E
L − H
values which lead to improving the open-circuit voltage. Due to the stabilization of LUMO and HOMO orbitals in GQD-PXIII, and Os-GQD-PXIII, the solvent effect induces larger
Δ
E
RP − H
and smaller
Δ
E
L − CB
. The hybridization in doping nanocomposite (Os-GQD-PXIIII) results in a significant red-shift of the absorbance in the Vis and IR regions and covered a wide region of the electromagnetic spectrum which shifted
J
sc
to a higher value compared to isolated dye. Os-GQD-PXIIII showed a better ability to transfer charge than the GQD-PXIIII and has a significantly longer excited-state lifetime of 122.495 ns. The solvent effect on altering the electronic properties (such as LHE, τ , and
λ
ss
) of photosensitizer molecule thus, in order to construct DSSC cells, the properties of the solvent must be taken into account. Additionally, the PXIII dye exhibits a small
J
sc
value because of its narrow absorbance range. While the two nanocomposites possess larger absorbances achieving significant increases in
J
sc
values. A huge difference in
J
sc
values between the isolated dye and hybridized systems will improve the performance DSSC. When DSSC is photosensitized with PXIII isolated dye, it is expected to have a smaller power conversion efficiency value than when DSSC is photosensitized with nanocomposites containing GQD-PXIII/Os-PXIII. By hybridizing PXIII dye with GQD, the adsorbed system becomes more stable. This may result from a charge redistribution within the nanocomposite framework, which enhances electron coupling between TiO2 semiconductor and adsorbed molecules. This work supported the design and screen of novel nanocomposite photosensitizers for DSSCs.
